scenario: Beacon retailer models reusable booking logic in Camunda 8.8 and the team handles 8000 orders per day across multiple parent processes. The architects consider collapsed subprocesses to simplify the diagram while still reusing the same internal flow.
question: Which BPMN element is recommended for creating reusable processes instead of collapsed subprocesses?

options.text: A collapsed subprocess linked from a drill-down icon enables reuse of the same internal flow across multiple parent processes
An embedded subprocess with a marked reusable flag exposes its internal elements to other parent processes for shared reuse
A call activity references a separately deployed process and is the documented way to model reusable processes across diagrams
An ad-hoc subprocess publishes its internal activities as a shared library that other parent processes can attach to at runtime

a.text: Incorrect. Collapsed subprocesses serve purely display purposes; the drill-down link only opens the embedded subprocess in the same diagram and does not enable reuse from other processes.

b.text: Incorrect. Embedded subprocesses are part of the parent process model and cannot be reused from other parent processes through a flag in Camunda 8.8.

c.text: Correct. Call activities reference a separately deployed process and are the documented way to create reusable processes when collapsed subprocesses only hide complexity in the diagram.

d.text: Incorrect. Ad-hoc subprocesses model unordered activities inside one process; they do not provide cross-process reuse of their internal flow.

explanation: Collapsed subprocesses only hide internal complexity in the diagram view and the drill-down link cannot target a different process. To create logic that is reusable across multiple parent processes, use a call activity that references a separately deployed process.
